original_hypothesis.title: SushiRouterSmol uses fixed 1 ETH amount regardless of input, enabling value confusion if accidentally configured in production paths
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: SushiRouterSmol’s swap functions ignore amountIn/msg.value economically and instead use a fixed `one = 1e18` for output/input, which can cause massive unintended value transfer if used as the swapRouter for an aggregator. If configured, attackers can exploit the mismatch to receive tokens/ETH disproportionate to what they provide.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a configuration-dependent but practical safety hypothesis: the code is clearly non-production-grade yet is present in-repo. If any deployment mistakenly points THORChain_Aggregator.swapRouter or other integrations to SushiRouterSmol, swaps become exploitable immediately.
deployment_assumptions: SushiRouterSmol is deployed and set as swapRouter for THORChain_Aggregator (or used by integrators as the DEX router)
WETH contract used by SushiRouterSmol has sufficient ETH reserves for withdrawals
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment scripts shown wiring THORChain_Aggregator; SushiRouterSmol exists as a contract in the repo and can be mistakenly used.

proof_of_signal.lines: swapExactTokensForETH(): amounts=[one]; withdraw(one) and send(one); swapExactETHForTokens(): deposit{value:one}() and transfer(one)

preconditions: A production aggregator/router integration uses SushiRouterSmol as swapRouter
Attacker can call the integrating swap function permissionlessly (e.g., aggregator.swapIn/swapOut)
SushiRouterSmol has access to WETH with ETH liquidity to pay out fixed 1 ETH

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: economic mismatch: ignores input amount, uses constant output
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: attacker gets 1 ETH worth per call for less input (or causes integrating contract to overpay)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no pricing, no pool accounting, no validation tying output to input

analysis_questions: Is SushiRouterSmol strictly test-only, or can it be deployed in environments used by integrators?
Are there guardrails in deployment tooling preventing non-canonical router addresses?
Could a chain environment accidentally substitute SushiRouterSmol at the expected router address (devnets/forks)?
validation_requirements: Review deployment configs for any reference to SushiRouterSmol
Simulate aggregator wired to SushiRouterSmol and quantify profit per call
Add deployment-time assertions verifying router bytecode hash/address is canonical

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If an aggregator uses SushiRouterSmol, attacker calls swapOut with minimal msg.value/amountIn yet receives 1e18 units output each time, draining the integrating contract/router’s ETH or token balances.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Severe (drain of funds routed through the misconfigured swap path)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users swapping through the misconfigured integration
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational only (redeploy/fix config); on-chain funds may already be drained

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No safeguards/tests ensuring production routers are canonical and economically correct

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor abnormal swap ratios and router address/bytecode hashes

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
